agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Integration architecture for a logistics platform connecting three external systems — SAP ERP (batch SFTP/XML), DHL Carrier API (REST/webhooks with 500ms SLA), and an Analytics Platform (real-time Kafka stream). The design uses an Anti-Corruption Layer per external system, an internal domain event bus as the central spine, and adapter components that translate between internal canonical events and each system's protocol, format, and timing requirements. This keeps the core domain clean while isolating each integration's complexity behind well-defined boundaries.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Core Domain Service","responsibility":"Owns the order and delivery lifecycle. Publishes canonical domain events (OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, ShipmentCreated, DeliveryAttempted, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed) to the internal event bus. Enforces all business invariants.","boundaries":"Never speaks external protocols. Only emits and consumes canonical domain events. No XML, no REST webhook handling, no Kafka producer logic lives here."},{"name":"Internal Event Bus","responsibility":"Durable, ordered backbone for all domain events. Decouples producers (Core Domain) from consumers (all three adapters). Provides at-least-once delivery guarantees and consumer group isolation so each adapter processes independently.","boundaries":"Internal only. Not directly exposed to any external system. Implementations can range from an in-process event dispatcher backed by a transactional outbox, to a dedicated internal Kafka topic or similar broker."},{"name":"SAP ERP Adapter (System A ACL)","responsibility":"Subscribes to OrderConfirmed events from the internal bus. Transforms canonical events into SAP-specific XML schema. Batches and delivers files via SFTP on a 15-minute schedule. Handles SFTP connection management, retry on transfer failure, file naming conventions, and delivery confirmation (e.g., checking for acknowledgment files).","boundaries":"Owns the SAP XML schema mapping and SFTP protocol concerns. The rest of the system is unaware of XML or SFTP. Outbound-only — no inbound data flow from SAP in current scope."},{"name":"DHL Carrier Adapter (System B ACL)","responsibility":"Two sub-components: (1) Outbound Client — subscribes to ShipmentCreated events and calls DHL REST API to book shipments, handling auth, rate limiting, retries, and circuit breaking. (2) Inbound Webhook Receiver — exposes an HTTP endpoint that DHL calls for status updates, validates signatures, responds within 500ms by immediately persisting the raw callback and publishing an internal event, then processes asynchronously. Translates DHL status codes into DeliveryAttempted, DeliveryCompleted, or DeliveryFailed domain events.","boundaries":"Owns DHL API contracts, authentication tokens, webhook signature validation. The core domain never sees DHL-specific data structures. The webhook endpoint is the only externally-exposed HTTP surface for this integration."},{"name":"Analytics Stream Adapter (System C ACL)","responsibility":"Subscribes to ALL domain events on the internal bus. Transforms each into the analytics platform's event schema. Publishes to external Kafka topics in real-time. Handles serialization (likely Avro or JSON Schema), schema registry integration, and producer error handling.","boundaries":"Owns the external Kafka producer configuration and analytics event schema. The core domain does not know about Kafka or the analytics schema. Read-only consumer of domain events — never writes back."},{"name":"Transactional Outbox","responsibility":"Guarantees that domain state changes and event publication are atomic. Domain events are written to an outbox table in the same transaction as the aggregate state change. A relay process (polling or CDC) publishes outbox entries to the internal event bus, ensuring no events are lost even if the bus is temporarily unavailable.","boundaries":"Lives at the persistence boundary of the Core Domain Service. Invisible to adapters — they only see events arriving on the internal bus."},{"name":"Dead Letter Queue + Monitoring","responsibility":"Captures events that any adapter fails to process after exhausting retries. Provides alerting, manual retry, and audit trail capabilities. Each adapter has its own DLQ to isolate failure domains.","boundaries":"Operational infrastructure. Does not affect domain logic. Per-adapter isolation prevents one system's failures from impacting others."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Core Domain Service","to":"Internal Event Bus","contract":"Publishes canonical domain events (OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, ShipmentCreated, DeliveryAttempted, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed) via transactional outbox. Events carry aggregate ID, timestamp, correlation ID, and event-specific payload.","coupling_type":"loose — event-driven, async"},{"from":"Internal Event Bus","to":"SAP ERP Adapter","contract":"Adapter subscribes to OrderConfirmed events. Consumes at its own pace via dedicated consumer group. Acknowledges only after successful batch write to SFTP.","coupling_type":"loose — async subscription"},{"from":"Internal Event Bus","to":"DHL Carrier Adapter (Outbound)","contract":"Adapter subscribes to ShipmentCreated events. Calls DHL REST API to create shipment. On failure, retries with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts before routing to DLQ.","coupling_type":"loose — async subscription, sync outbound HTTP to DHL"},{"from":"DHL Webhook Endpoint","to":"DHL Carrier Adapter (Inbound)","contract":"DHL POSTs shipment status callbacks. Adapter validates HMAC signature, persists raw payload, returns 200 OK within 500ms. Async worker then translates to domain events and publishes to internal bus.","coupling_type":"temporal — must respond within 500ms SLA"},{"from":"DHL Carrier Adapter (Inbound)","to":"Core Domain Service","contract":"Publishes translated DeliveryAttempted / DeliveryCompleted / DeliveryFailed events via the internal bus, or invokes a command on the Core Domain Service to update aggregate state, which in turn emits the canonical domain event.","coupling_type":"loose — event-driven or command-driven"},{"from":"Internal Event Bus","to":"Analytics Stream Adapter","contract":"Adapter subscribes to ALL domain events. Transforms each to analytics schema and produces to external Kafka topic. Uses a separate consumer group so its throughput and failures are independent.","coupling_type":"loose — async subscription, async Kafka produce"}],"data_flow":"1. Core Domain Service processes commands → writes aggregate state + outbox entry in one transaction. 2. Outbox Relay publishes events to Internal Event Bus. 3. SAP Adapter: consumes OrderConfirmed → accumulates in a 15-min batch window → transforms to XML → writes to SFTP. 4. DHL Outbound Adapter: consumes ShipmentCreated → calls DHL REST API → receives booking confirmation. 5. DHL Inbound: DHL webhook → fast-ack (persist raw + 200 OK) → async worker translates → publishes command/event to Core Domain → Core Domain updates aggregate → outbox → bus → all adapters see the delivery status event. 6. Analytics Adapter: consumes every domain event → transforms to analytics schema → publishes to external Kafka topic in real time.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Anti-Corruption Layer (ACL)","rationale":"Each external system has its own data model, protocol, and timing. ACLs prevent external concerns from leaking into the core domain. SAP's XML, DHL's REST contracts, and the analytics Kafka schema are all isolated behind translation layers."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Guarantees atomicity between domain state changes and event publication. Without this, we risk ghost events (event published but state not committed) or lost events (state committed but event never published). Critical for correctness across all three integrations."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture","rationale":"The internal event bus decouples the core domain from all three consumers. Each adapter operates independently — SAP batching on a 15-min window, DHL processing per-event, Analytics streaming in real time — without the core knowing or caring."},{"pattern":"Webhook Fast-Ack with Async Processing","rationale":"DHL's 500ms SLA on webhook response time demands that we separate acknowledgment from processing. We persist the raw payload and return 200 immediately, then process asynchronously. This prevents downstream slowness from violating the SLA."},{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker","rationale":"Applied to the DHL outbound REST client. If DHL's API degrades, the circuit breaker prevents cascading timeouts and allows graceful degradation with queued retries."},{"pattern":"Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"Per-adapter DLQs isolate failure domains. A malformed SAP XML mapping failure doesn't block DHL shipment creation or analytics streaming. Enables manual investigation and replay."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId) — states: Placed → Confirmed → Shipped. Invariants: cannot confirm a cancelled order, cannot ship an unconfirmed order."]},{"name":"Shipping & Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["Shipment (root: ShipmentId, ref: OrderId) — states: Created → InTransit → Delivered / Failed. Invariants: delivery status transitions must be valid (no Delivered → InTransit), max delivery attempts enforced."]},{"name":"ERP Integration","classification":"generic","aggregates":["OutboundERPFile (root: FileId) — tracks batch assembly, SFTP transfer status, acknowledgment. No business logic — purely integration bookkeeping."]},{"name":"Carrier Integration","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CarrierBooking (root: BookingId, ref: ShipmentId) — tracks DHL-specific booking reference, API call status, webhook correlation. Translates DHL-specific statuses to domain events."]},{"name":"Analytics Integration","classification":"generic","aggregates":["No aggregates — stateless event transformer and producer. Schema mapping configuration only."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"ERP Integration","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Management publishes OrderConfirmed; ERP Integration conforms to the event schema and transforms to SAP XML."},{"upstream":"Shipping & Delivery","downstream":"Carrier Integration","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Shipping publishes ShipmentCreated; Carrier Integration translates to DHL API calls. Inbound: Carrier Integration translates DHL callbacks and publishes commands back upstream."},{"upstream":"Carrier Integration","downstream":"Shipping & Delivery","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer — DHL-specific statuses are translated to canonical delivery events before entering the Shipping context. The ACL prevents DHL's model from polluting the domain."},{"upstream":"Order Management + Shipping & Delivery","downstream":"Analytics Integration","relationship":"Conformist — Analytics conforms to all canonical domain events and transforms them for the external platform. No upstream impact."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Internal event bus as central spine rather than point-to-point integrations","gains":"Complete decoupling between core domain and all three external systems. Adding a fourth integration requires zero changes to existing code. Each adapter scales, fails, and evolves independently.","sacrifices":"Adds operational complexity (bus infrastructure, monitoring, consumer group management). Introduces eventual consistency — SAP file won't reflect an order for up to 15 minutes + bus propagation delay.","alternatives_considered":["Direct service calls from domain to each adapter — simpler but creates tight coupling and makes adding new integrations expensive","Shared external Kafka as the internal bus — reduces infrastructure but couples internal architecture to an external system's availability and schema"]},{"decision":"Webhook fast-ack pattern for DHL callbacks","gains":"Guarantees 500ms SLA compliance regardless of downstream processing time. Raw payload persistence provides an audit trail and replay capability.","sacrifices":"Two-phase processing adds complexity. There's a window where the webhook is ack'd but the domain event hasn't been published yet — requires idempotency handling if DHL retries.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous end-to-end processing in webhook handler — simpler but risks SLA breach if domain logic or event publishing is slow","Queue-based ingestion without fast-ack — webhook would time out waiting for queue confirmation"]},{"decision":"Transactional outbox over dual-write to event bus","gains":"Eliminates dual-write inconsistency. Guarantees exactly-once event publication per state change (with idempotent consumers for at-least-once delivery).","sacrifices":"Adds outbox table and relay process. Introduces small latency between state change and event availability on the bus (typically <100ms with polling, near-zero with CDC).","alternatives_considered":["Direct publish to event bus in same request — risks lost events on bus failure","Change Data Capture on domain tables — avoids outbox table but couples event schema to database schema"]},{"decision":"Per-adapter DLQ isolation","gains":"Failure in one integration channel cannot impact others. Clear ownership of failure investigation per team/adapter.","sacrifices":"More infrastructure to manage — three DLQs instead of one. Requires per-adapter alerting configuration.","alternatives_considered":["Single shared DLQ — simpler but a flood of SAP failures could mask DHL failures"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new external integrations","evolution_path":"New adapter subscribes to internal event bus with its own consumer group. Zero changes to core domain or existing adapters. Could add a warehouse management system, customer notification service, or additional carrier in the same pattern."},{"area":"Changing carrier from DHL to another provider","evolution_path":"Replace the DHL Carrier Adapter with a new adapter implementing the same internal event contracts. Core domain and other adapters are unaffected. Can run both in parallel during migration."},{"area":"SAP batch frequency","evolution_path":"The 15-minute batch window is a configuration parameter in the SAP adapter. Can be changed to 5 minutes or 30 minutes without architectural changes. Could even switch to real-time single-event SFTP if SAP supports it."},{"area":"Analytics platform migration","evolution_path":"The analytics adapter owns the external Kafka producer. Switching to a different streaming platform (Kinesis, Pulsar, direct HTTP) only requires changing this adapter. The event schema mapping layer can be reused."},{"area":"Event schema evolution","evolution_path":"Schema registry for both internal and external events. Backward-compatible changes (adding optional fields) propagate without adapter changes. Breaking changes require versioned event types and adapter updates."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Core domain event contracts","reason":"All three integrations depend on the canonical domain events (OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, etc.). Changing event names, removing fields, or altering semantics requires coordinated updates across all adapters. This rigidity is intentional — the canonical model IS the stable contract."},{"area":"At-least-once delivery semantics","reason":"All adapters must be idempotent because the event bus guarantees at-least-once, not exactly-once. This is a fundamental architectural constraint that cannot be relaxed without changing the bus infrastructure."},{"area":"Transactional outbox relay","reason":"The outbox relay is a critical infrastructure component. If it stops, all integrations stop receiving events. Must be highly available and monitored. Changing the relay mechanism (polling vs CDC) requires careful migration."}],"risks":[{"risk":"DHL webhook 500ms SLA breach under load","severity":"high","mitigation":"Fast-ack pattern ensures response time is bounded by persist latency only (~5-20ms). Load test the webhook endpoint independently. Use a dedicated thread pool / process for webhook handling, isolated from other processing. Monitor p99 response times with alerting at 400ms."},{"risk":"SAP SFTP batch delivery failure with no retry causes data loss","severity":"high","mitigation":"Track each batch file's delivery status in the OutboundERPFile aggregate. Implement exponential backoff retry (3 attempts over 15 minutes). On final failure, route to DLQ and alert operations. Keep the source events on the bus so the batch can be rebuilt from events."},{"risk":"Internal event bus becomes single point of failure","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy the event bus infrastructure (whether internal broker or outbox relay) with high availability — clustered, replicated, with automated failover. The transactional outbox acts as a buffer: if the bus is down, events accumulate in the outbox and are published when the bus recovers. Monitor bus health as a top-level SLA."},{"risk":"Schema drift between internal events and external system expectations","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a schema registry for internal events. Version all external adapter schemas. Automated contract tests per adapter that validate transformation correctness. Break the build on incompatible schema changes."},{"risk":"Ordering guarantees lost across adapters","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Partition internal events by aggregate ID (OrderId / ShipmentId) to guarantee per-entity ordering. Each adapter's consumer respects partition ordering. Document that cross-entity ordering is NOT guaranteed — adapters must not depend on it."},{"risk":"DHL outbound API degradation causes shipment booking backlog","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Circuit breaker on DHL client with fallback to queued retry. Exponential backoff with jitter. Dashboard showing booking queue depth and age. Alert when oldest unprocessed ShipmentCreated event exceeds 5 minutes."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement in three phases. Phase 1: Core domain events + transactional outbox + internal event bus. This is the foundation — nothing works without reliable event flow. Phase 2: DHL adapter (highest complexity due to bidirectional communication and SLA constraint) — start with the webhook fast-ack receiver, then the outbound client. Phase 3: SAP adapter (batch complexity but no real-time pressure) and Analytics adapter (simplest — stateless event transformation) in parallel. Validate each phase with integration tests that simulate external system behavior using WireMock (DHL), local SFTP (SAP), and embedded Kafka (Analytics).","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/domain/events/ — canonical event definitions (OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, ShipmentCreated, DeliveryAttempted, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed)","src/domain/order/ — Order aggregate, OrderRepository interface","src/domain/shipping/ — Shipment aggregate, ShipmentRepository interface","src/infrastructure/outbox/ — OutboxEntry entity, OutboxRelay (polling or CDC-based publisher)","src/infrastructure/eventbus/ — Internal event bus abstraction + implementation","src/adapters/sap/ — SAPOrderConfirmedHandler, XMLTransformer, SFTPClient, BatchScheduler","src/adapters/dhl/outbound/ — DHLShipmentClient, CircuitBreakerConfig, DHLRequestMapper","src/adapters/dhl/inbound/ — WebhookController, RawPayloadRepository, WebhookAsyncProcessor, DHLStatusTranslator","src/adapters/analytics/ — AnalyticsEventPublisher, KafkaProducerConfig, AnalyticsEventMapper","src/infrastructure/dlq/ — DeadLetterQueue per adapter, DLQMonitor","tests/integration/adapters/ — per-adapter integration tests with external system simulators"],"patterns_to_follow":"Hexagonal Architecture (Ports & Adapters) — domain at the center, adapters at the edges. Each adapter implements a port interface defined by the domain. Strict dependency rule: adapters depend on domain, never the reverse. Use dependency injection to wire adapters. All external communication happens through adapter implementations, never directly from domain code.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["DO NOT let DHL-specific data structures leak into the core domain — always translate through the ACL","DO NOT process DHL webhooks synchronously end-to-end — the 500ms SLA will be violated under load","DO NOT share a single consumer group across adapters — one slow adapter will block all others","DO NOT skip the transactional outbox — dual-writing to DB and event bus will eventually lose events","DO NOT couple the analytics Kafka producer to the internal event bus implementation — they are separate concerns even if both use Kafka","DO NOT batch DHL API calls to match SAP's 15-minute rhythm — DHL integration should be near-real-time per-event","DO NOT ignore idempotency — at-least-once delivery means every adapter will occasionally see duplicate events"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Anti-Corruption Layer per external system is clearly the right boundary pattern given three systems with different protocols, schemas, and timing","Transactional outbox is the correct solution for reliable event publishing — well-established pattern with known trade-offs","Webhook fast-ack pattern is necessary and sufficient for the 500ms DHL SLA","Event-driven internal architecture is the natural fit for fan-out to three independent consumers"],"low_confidence_areas":["Internal event bus technology choice (in-process outbox relay vs. dedicated broker like Kafka/RabbitMQ) — depends on scale requirements not specified","Whether SAP integration is truly outbound-only or if there will be inbound purchase orders or inventory updates in the future","Exact DHL webhook payload structure and retry behavior — would need API documentation to finalize the ACL translation layer","Whether the analytics platform requires exactly-once semantics or can tolerate at-least-once with deduplication"],"assumptions":["The logistics platform is a single deployable unit (monolith or modular monolith), not a microservices system — if it's microservices, the internal event bus becomes an inter-service broker with additional concerns","SAP integration is outbound-only (confirmed orders TO SAP), no inbound data flow from SAP in current scope","DHL provides webhook signature validation (HMAC or similar) for security","The analytics Kafka cluster is externally managed — the platform only produces to it","Event volume is moderate (thousands per hour, not millions) — at higher volumes, the outbox polling approach may need to be replaced with CDC"]}}

The design uses an Anti-Corruption Layer per external system, an internal domain event bus as the central spine, and adapter components that translate between internal canonical events and each system's protocol, format, and timing requirements. content.thinking:
Let me design the integration architecture for this logistics platform with three external systems.

Classification: `none` — sufficient constraints exist. I have clear NFRs (500ms webhook response, 15-min SFTP batch, real-time Kafka stream), integration boundaries (SAP/ERP, DHL/Carrier, Analytics), and domain events defined.

Raw payload persistence provides an audit trail and replay capability.","sacrifices":"Two-phase processing adds complexity. There's a window where the webhook is ack'd but the domain event hasn't been published yet — requires idempotency handling if DHL retries.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous end-to-end processing in webhook handler — simpler but risks SLA breach if domain logic or event publishing is slow","Queue-based ingestion without fast-ack — webhook would time out waiting for queue confirmation"]},{"decision":"Transactional outbox over dual-write to event bus","gains":"Eliminates dual-write inconsistency. Guarantees exactly-once event publication per state change (with idempotent consumers for at-least-once delivery).","sacrifices":"Adds outbox table and relay process. Introduces small latency between state change and event availability on the bus (typically <100ms with polling, near-zero with CDC).","alternatives_considered":["Direct publish to event bus in same request — risks lost events on bus failure","Change Data Capture on domain tables — avoids outbox table but couples event schema to database schema"]},{"decision":"Per-adapter DLQ isolation","gains":"Failure in one integration channel cannot impact others. Clear ownership of failure investigation per team/adapter.","sacrifices":"More infrastructure to manage — three DLQs instead of one. Requires per-adapter alerting configuration.","alternatives_considered":["Single shared DLQ — simpler but a flood of SAP failures could mask DHL failures"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new external integrations","evolution_path":"New adapter subscribes to internal event bus with its own consumer group. Zero changes to core domain or existing adapters. Could add a warehouse management system, customer notification service, or additional carrier in the same pattern."},{"area":"Changing carrier from DHL to another provider","evolution_path":"Replace the DHL Carrier Adapter with a new adapter implementing the same internal event contracts. Core domain and other adapters are unaffected. Can run both in parallel during migration."},{"area":"SAP batch frequency","evolution_path":"The 15-minute batch window is a configuration parameter in the SAP adapter. Can be changed to 5 minutes or 30 minutes without architectural changes. Could even switch to real-time single-event SFTP if SAP supports it."},{"area":"Analytics platform migration","evolution_path":"The analytics adapter owns the external Kafka producer. Switching to a different streaming platform (Kinesis, Pulsar, direct HTTP) only requires changing this adapter. The event schema mapping layer can be reused."},{"area":"Event schema evolution","evolution_path":"Schema registry for both internal and external events. Backward-compatible changes (adding optional fields) propagate without adapter changes. Breaking changes require versioned event types and adapter updates."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Core domain event contracts","reason":"All three integrations depend on the canonical domain events (OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, etc.). Changing event names, removing fields, or altering semantics requires coordinated updates across all adapters. This rigidity is intentional — the canonical model IS the stable contract."},{"area":"At-least-once delivery semantics","reason":"All adapters must be idempotent because the event bus guarantees at-least-once, not exactly-once. This is a fundamental architectural constraint that cannot be relaxed without changing the bus infrastructure."},{"area":"Transactional outbox relay","reason":"The outbox relay is a critical infrastructure component. If it stops, all integrations stop receiving events. Must be highly available and monitored. Changing the relay mechanism (polling vs CDC) requires careful migration."}],"risks":[{"risk":"DHL webhook 500ms SLA breach under load","severity":"high","mitigation":"Fast-ack pattern ensures response time is bounded by persist latency only (~5-20ms). Load test the webhook endpoint independently. Use a dedicated thread pool / process for webhook handling, isolated from other processing. Monitor p99 response times with alerting at 400ms."},{"risk":"SAP SFTP batch delivery failure with no retry causes data loss","severity":"high","mitigation":"Track each batch file's delivery status in the OutboundERPFile aggregate. Implement exponential backoff retry (3 attempts over 15 minutes). On final failure, route to DLQ and alert operations. Keep the source events on the bus so the batch can be rebuilt from events."},{"risk":"Internal event bus becomes single point of failure","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy the event bus infrastructure (whether internal broker or outbox relay) with high availability — clustered, replicated, with automated failover. The transactional outbox acts as a buffer: if the bus is down, events accumulate in the outbox and are published when the bus recovers. Monitor bus health as a top-level SLA."},{"risk":"Schema drift between internal events and external system expectations","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a schema registry for internal events. Version all external adapter schemas. Automated contract tests per adapter that validate transformation correctness. Break the build on incompatible schema changes."},{"risk":"Ordering guarantees lost across adapters","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Partition internal events by aggregate ID (OrderId / ShipmentId) to guarantee per-entity ordering. Each adapter's consumer respects partition ordering. Document that cross-entity ordering is NOT guaranteed — adapters must not depend on it."},{"risk":"DHL outbound API degradation causes shipment booking backlog","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Circuit breaker on DHL client with fallback to queued retry. Exponential backoff with jitter. Dashboard showing booking queue depth and age. Alert when oldest unprocessed ShipmentCreated event exceeds 5 minutes."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement in three phases. Phase 1: Core domain events + transactional outbox + internal event bus. This is the foundation — nothing works without reliable event flow. Phase 2: DHL adapter (highest complexity due to bidirectional communication and SLA constraint) — start with the webhook fast-ack receiver, then the outbound client. Phase 3: SAP adapter (batch complexity but no real-time pressure) and Analytics adapter (simplest — stateless event transformation) in parallel. Validate each phase with integration tests that simulate external system behavior using WireMock (DHL), local SFTP (SAP), and embedded Kafka (Analytics).","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/domain/events/ — canonical event definitions (OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, ShipmentCreated, DeliveryAttempted, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed)","src/domain/order/ — Order aggregate, OrderRepository interface","src/domain/shipping/ — Shipment aggregate, ShipmentRepository interface","src/infrastructure/outbox/ — OutboxEntry entity, OutboxRelay (polling or CDC-based publisher)","src/infrastructure/eventbus/ — Internal event bus abstraction + implementation","src/adapters/sap/ — SAPOrderConfirmedHandler, XMLTransformer, SFTPClient, BatchScheduler","src/adapters/dhl/outbound/ — DHLShipmentClient, CircuitBreakerConfig, DHLRequestMapper","src/adapters/dhl/inbound/ — WebhookController, RawPayloadRepository, WebhookAsyncProcessor, DHLStatusTranslator","src/adapters/analytics/ — AnalyticsEventPublisher, KafkaProducerConfig, AnalyticsEventMapper","src/infrastructure/dlq/ — DeadLetterQueue per adapter, DLQMonitor","tests/integration/adapters/ — per-adapter integration tests with external system simulators"],"patterns_to_follow":"Hexagonal Architecture (Ports & Adapters) — domain at the center, adapters at the edges. Each adapter implements a port interface defined by the domain. Strict dependency rule: adapters depend on domain, never the reverse. Use dependency injection to wire adapters. All external communication happens through adapter implementations, never directly from domain code.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["DO NOT let DHL-specific data structures leak into the core domain — always translate through the ACL","DO NOT process DHL webhooks synchronously end-to-end — the 500ms SLA will be violated under load","DO NOT share a single consumer group across adapters — one slow adapter will block all others","DO NOT skip the transactional outbox — dual-writing to DB and event bus will eventually lose events","DO NOT couple the analytics Kafka producer to the internal event bus implementation — they are separate concerns even if both use Kafka","DO NOT batch DHL API calls to match SAP's 15-minute rhythm — DHL integration should be near-real-time per-event","DO NOT ignore idempotency — at-least-once delivery means every adapter will occasionally see duplicate events"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Anti-Corruption Layer per external system is clearly the right boundary pattern given three systems with different protocols, schemas, and timing","Transactional outbox is the correct solution for reliable event publishing — well-established pattern with known trade-offs","Webhook fast-ack pattern is necessary and sufficient for the 500ms DHL SLA","Event-driven internal architecture is the natural fit for fan-out to three independent consumers"],"low_confidence_areas":["Internal event bus technology choice (in-process outbox relay vs. dedicated broker like Kafka/RabbitMQ) — depends on scale requirements not specified","Whether SAP integration is truly outbound-only or if there will be inbound purchase orders or inventory updates in the future","Exact DHL webhook payload structure and retry behavior — would need API documentation to finalize the ACL translation layer","Whether the analytics platform requires exactly-once semantics or can tolerate at-least-once with deduplication"],"assumptions":["The logistics platform is a single deployable unit (monolith or modular monolith), not a microservices system — if it's microservices, the internal event bus becomes an inter-service broker with additional concerns","SAP integration is outbound-only (confirmed orders TO SAP), no inbound data flow from SAP in current scope","DHL provides webhook signature validation (HMAC or similar) for security","The analytics Kafka cluster is externally managed — the platform only produces to it","Event volume is moderate (thousands per hour, not millions) — at higher volumes, the outbox polling approach may need to be replaced with CDC"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"Integration architecture for a logistics platform connecting three external systems — SAP ERP (batch SFTP/XML), DHL Carrier API (REST/webhooks with 500ms SLA), and an Analytics Platform (real-time Kafka stream). The design uses an Anti-Corruption Layer per external system, an internal domain event bus as the central spine, and adapter components that translate between internal canonical events and each system's protocol, format, and timing requirements. This keeps the core domain clean while isolating each integration's complexity behind well-defined boundaries.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Core Domain Service","responsibility":"Owns the order and delivery lifecycle. Publishes canonical domain events (OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, ShipmentCreated, DeliveryAttempted, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed) to the internal event bus. Enforces all business invariants.","boundaries":"Never speaks external protocols. Only emits and consumes canonical domain events. No XML, no REST webhook handling, no Kafka producer logic lives here."},{"name":"Internal Event Bus","responsibility":"Durable, ordered backbone for all domain events. Decouples producers (Core Domain) from consumers (all three adapters). Provides at-least-once delivery guarantees and consumer group isolation so each adapter processes independently.","boundaries":"Internal only. Not directly exposed to any external system. Implementations can range from an in-process event dispatcher backed by a transactional outbox, to a dedicated internal Kafka topic or similar broker."},{"name":"SAP ERP Adapter (System A ACL)","responsibility":"Subscribes to OrderConfirmed events from the internal bus. Transforms canonical events into SAP-specific XML schema. Batches and delivers files via SFTP on a 15-minute schedule. Handles SFTP connection management, retry on transfer failure, file naming conventions, and delivery confirmation (e.g., checking for acknowledgment files).","boundaries":"Owns the SAP XML schema mapping and SFTP protocol concerns. The rest of the system is unaware of XML or SFTP. Outbound-only — no inbound data flow from SAP in current scope."},{"name":"DHL Carrier Adapter (System B ACL)","responsibility":"Two sub-components: (1) Outbound Client — subscribes to ShipmentCreated events and calls DHL REST API to book shipments, handling auth, rate limiting, retries, and circuit breaking. (2) Inbound Webhook Receiver — exposes an HTTP endpoint that DHL calls for status updates, validates signatures, responds within 500ms by immediately persisting the raw callback and publishing an internal event, then processes asynchronously. Translates DHL status codes into DeliveryAttempted, DeliveryCompleted, or DeliveryFailed domain events.","boundaries":"Owns DHL API contracts, authentication tokens, webhook signature validation. The core domain never sees DHL-specific data structures. The webhook endpoint is the only externally-exposed HTTP surface for this integration."},{"name":"Analytics Stream Adapter (System C ACL)","responsibility":"Subscribes to ALL domain events on the internal bus. Transforms each into the analytics platform's event schema. Publishes to external Kafka topics in real-time. Handles serialization (likely Avro or JSON Schema), schema registry integration, and producer error handling.","boundaries":"Owns the external Kafka producer configuration and analytics event schema. The core domain does not know about Kafka or the analytics schema. Read-only consumer of domain events — never writes back."},{"name":"Transactional Outbox","responsibility":"Guarantees that domain state changes and event publication are atomic. Domain events are written to an outbox table in the same transaction as the aggregate state change. A relay process (polling or CDC) publishes outbox entries to the internal event bus, ensuring no events are lost even if the bus is temporarily unavailable.","boundaries":"Lives at the persistence boundary of the Core Domain Service. Invisible to adapters — they only see events arriving on the internal bus."},{"name":"Dead Letter Queue + Monitoring","responsibility":"Captures events that any adapter fails to process after exhausting retries. Provides alerting, manual retry, and audit trail capabilities. Each adapter has its own DLQ to isolate failure domains.","boundaries":"Operational infrastructure. Does not affect domain logic. Per-adapter isolation prevents one system's failures from impacting others."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Core Domain Service","to":"Internal Event Bus","contract":"Publishes canonical domain events (OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, ShipmentCreated, DeliveryAttempted, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed) via transactional outbox. Events carry aggregate ID, timestamp, correlation ID, and event-specific payload.","coupling_type":"loose — event-driven, async"},{"from":"Internal Event Bus","to":"SAP ERP Adapter","contract":"Adapter subscribes to OrderConfirmed events. Consumes at its own pace via dedicated consumer group. Acknowledges only after successful batch write to SFTP.","coupling_type":"loose — async subscription"},{"from":"Internal Event Bus","to":"DHL Carrier Adapter (Outbound)","contract":"Adapter subscribes to ShipmentCreated events. Calls DHL REST API to create shipment. On failure, retries with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts before routing to DLQ.","coupling_type":"loose — async subscription, sync outbound HTTP to DHL"},{"from":"DHL Webhook Endpoint","to":"DHL Carrier Adapter (Inbound)","contract":"DHL POSTs shipment status callbacks. Adapter validates HMAC signature, persists raw payload, returns 200 OK within 500ms. Async worker then translates to domain events and publishes to internal bus.","coupling_type":"temporal — must respond within 500ms SLA"},{"from":"DHL Carrier Adapter (Inbound)","to":"Core Domain Service","contract":"Publishes translated DeliveryAttempted / DeliveryCompleted / DeliveryFailed events via the internal bus, or invokes a command on the Core Domain Service to update aggregate state, which in turn emits the canonical domain event.","coupling_type":"loose — event-driven or command-driven"},{"from":"Internal Event Bus","to":"Analytics Stream Adapter","contract":"Adapter subscribes to ALL domain events. Transforms each to analytics schema and produces to external Kafka topic. Uses a separate consumer group so its throughput and failures are independent.","coupling_type":"loose — async subscription, async Kafka produce"}],"data_flow":"1. Core Domain Service processes commands → writes aggregate state + outbox entry in one transaction. 2. Outbox Relay publishes events to Internal Event Bus. 3. SAP Adapter: consumes OrderConfirmed → accumulates in a 15-min batch window → transforms to XML → writes to SFTP. 4. DHL Outbound Adapter: consumes ShipmentCreated → calls DHL REST API → receives booking confirmation. 5. DHL Inbound: DHL webhook → fast-ack (persist raw + 200 OK) → async worker translates → publishes command/event to Core Domain → Core Domain updates aggregate → outbox → bus → all adapters see the delivery status event. 6. Analytics Adapter: consumes every domain event → transforms to analytics schema → publishes to external Kafka topic in real time.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Anti-Corruption Layer (ACL)","rationale":"Each external system has its own data model, protocol, and timing. ACLs prevent external concerns from leaking into the core domain. SAP's XML, DHL's REST contracts, and the analytics Kafka schema are all isolated behind translation layers."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Guarantees atomicity between domain state changes and event publication. Without this, we risk ghost events (event published but state not committed) or lost events (state committed but event never published). Critical for correctness across all three integrations."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture","rationale":"The internal event bus decouples the core domain from all three consumers. Each adapter operates independently — SAP batching on a 15-min window, DHL processing per-event, Analytics streaming in real time — without the core knowing or caring."},{"pattern":"Webhook Fast-Ack with Async Processing","rationale":"DHL's 500ms SLA on webhook response time demands that we separate acknowledgment from processing. We persist the raw payload and return 200 immediately, then process asynchronously. This prevents downstream slowness from violating the SLA."},{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker","rationale":"Applied to the DHL outbound REST client. If DHL's API degrades, the circuit breaker prevents cascading timeouts and allows graceful degradation with queued retries."},{"pattern":"Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"Per-adapter DLQs isolate failure domains. A malformed SAP XML mapping failure doesn't block DHL shipment creation or analytics streaming. Enables manual investigation and replay."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId) — states: Placed → Confirmed → Shipped. Invariants: cannot confirm a cancelled order, cannot ship an unconfirmed order."]},{"name":"Shipping & Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["Shipment (root: ShipmentId, ref: OrderId) — states: Created → InTransit → Delivered / Failed. Invariants: delivery status transitions must be valid (no Delivered → InTransit), max delivery attempts enforced."]},{"name":"ERP Integration","classification":"generic","aggregates":["OutboundERPFile (root: FileId) — tracks batch assembly, SFTP transfer status, acknowledgment. No business logic — purely integration bookkeeping."]},{"name":"Carrier Integration","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CarrierBooking (root: BookingId, ref: ShipmentId) — tracks DHL-specific booking reference, API call status, webhook correlation. Translates DHL-specific statuses to domain events."]},{"name":"Analytics Integration","classification":"generic","aggregates":["No aggregates — stateless event transformer and producer. Schema mapping configuration only."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"ERP Integration","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Management publishes OrderConfirmed; ERP Integration conforms to the event schema and transforms to SAP XML."},{"upstream":"Shipping & Delivery","downstream":"Carrier Integration","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Shipping publishes ShipmentCreated; Carrier Integration translates to DHL API calls. Inbound: Carrier Integration translates DHL callbacks and publishes commands back upstream."},{"upstream":"Carrier Integration","downstream":"Shipping & Delivery","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer — DHL-specific statuses are translated to canonical delivery events before entering the Shipping context. The ACL prevents DHL's model from polluting the domain."},{"upstream":"Order Management + Shipping & Delivery","downstream":"Analytics Integration","relationship":"Conformist — Analytics conforms to all canonical domain events and transforms them for the external platform. No upstream impact."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Internal event bus as central spine rather than point-to-point integrations","gains":"Complete decoupling between core domain and all three external systems. Adding a fourth integration requires zero changes to existing code. Each adapter scales, fails, and evolves independently.","sacrifices":"Adds operational complexity (bus infrastructure, monitoring, consumer group management). Introduces eventual consistency — SAP file won't reflect an order for up to 15 minutes + bus propagation delay.","alternatives_considered":["Direct service calls from domain to each adapter — simpler but creates tight coupling and makes adding new integrations expensive","Shared external Kafka as the internal bus — reduces infrastructure but couples internal architecture to an external system's availability and schema"]},{"decision":"Webhook fast-ack pattern for DHL callbacks","gains":"Guarantees 500ms SLA compliance regardless of downstream processing time. Raw payload persistence provides an audit trail and replay capability.","sacrifices":"Two-phase processing adds complexity. There's a window where the webhook is ack'd but the domain event hasn't been published yet — requires idempotency handling if DHL retries.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous end-to-end processing in webhook handler — simpler but risks SLA breach if domain logic or event publishing is slow","Queue-based ingestion without fast-ack — webhook would time out waiting for queue confirmation"]},{"decision":"Transactional outbox over dual-write to event bus","gains":"Eliminates dual-write inconsistency. Guarantees exactly-once event publication per state change (with idempotent consumers for at-least-once delivery).","sacrifices":"Adds outbox table and relay process. Introduces small latency between state change and event availability on the bus (typically <100ms with polling, near-zero with CDC).","alternatives_considered":["Direct publish to event bus in same request — risks lost events on bus failure","Change Data Capture on domain tables — avoids outbox table but couples event schema to database schema"]},{"decision":"Per-adapter DLQ isolation","gains":"Failure in one integration channel cannot impact others. Clear ownership of failure investigation per team/adapter.","sacrifices":"More infrastructure to manage — three DLQs instead of one. Requires per-adapter alerting configuration.","alternatives_considered":["Single shared DLQ — simpler but a flood of SAP failures could mask DHL failures"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new external integrations","evolution_path":"New adapter subscribes to internal event bus with its own consumer group. Zero changes to core domain or existing adapters. Could add a warehouse management system, customer notification service, or additional carrier in the same pattern."},{"area":"Changing carrier from DHL to another provider","evolution_path":"Replace the DHL Carrier Adapter with a new adapter implementing the same internal event contracts. Core domain and other adapters are unaffected. Can run both in parallel during migration."},{"area":"SAP batch frequency","evolution_path":"The 15-minute batch window is a configuration parameter in the SAP adapter. Can be changed to 5 minutes or 30 minutes without architectural changes. Could even switch to real-time single-event SFTP if SAP supports it."},{"area":"Analytics platform migration","evolution_path":"The analytics adapter owns the external Kafka producer. Switching to a different streaming platform (Kinesis, Pulsar, direct HTTP) only requires changing this adapter. The event schema mapping layer can be reused."},{"area":"Event schema evolution","evolution_path":"Schema registry for both internal and external events. Backward-compatible changes (adding optional fields) propagate without adapter changes. Breaking changes require versioned event types and adapter updates."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Core domain event contracts","reason":"All three integrations depend on the canonical domain events (OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, etc.). Changing event names, removing fields, or altering semantics requires coordinated updates across all adapters. This rigidity is intentional — the canonical model IS the stable contract."},{"area":"At-least-once delivery semantics","reason":"All adapters must be idempotent because the event bus guarantees at-least-once, not exactly-once. This is a fundamental architectural constraint that cannot be relaxed without changing the bus infrastructure."},{"area":"Transactional outbox relay","reason":"The outbox relay is a critical infrastructure component. If it stops, all integrations stop receiving events. Must be highly available and monitored. Changing the relay mechanism (polling vs CDC) requires careful migration."}],"risks":[{"risk":"DHL webhook 500ms SLA breach under load","severity":"high","mitigation":"Fast-ack pattern ensures response time is bounded by persist latency only (~5-20ms). Load test the webhook endpoint independently. Use a dedicated thread pool / process for webhook handling, isolated from other processing. Monitor p99 response times with alerting at 400ms."},{"risk":"SAP SFTP batch delivery failure with no retry causes data loss","severity":"high","mitigation":"Track each batch file's delivery status in the OutboundERPFile aggregate. Implement exponential backoff retry (3 attempts over 15 minutes). On final failure, route to DLQ and alert operations. Keep the source events on the bus so the batch can be rebuilt from events."},{"risk":"Internal event bus becomes single point of failure","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy the event bus infrastructure (whether internal broker or outbox relay) with high availability — clustered, replicated, with automated failover. The transactional outbox acts as a buffer: if the bus is down, events accumulate in the outbox and are published when the bus recovers. Monitor bus health as a top-level SLA."},{"risk":"Schema drift between internal events and external system expectations","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a schema registry for internal events. Version all external adapter schemas. Automated contract tests per adapter that validate transformation correctness. Break the build on incompatible schema changes."},{"risk":"Ordering guarantees lost across adapters","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Partition internal events by aggregate ID (OrderId / ShipmentId) to guarantee per-entity ordering. Each adapter's consumer respects partition ordering. Document that cross-entity ordering is NOT guaranteed — adapters must not depend on it."},{"risk":"DHL outbound API degradation causes shipment booking backlog","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Circuit breaker on DHL client with fallback to queued retry. Exponential backoff with jitter. Dashboard showing booking queue depth and age. Alert when oldest unprocessed ShipmentCreated event exceeds 5 minutes."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement in three phases. Phase 1: Core domain events + transactional outbox + internal event bus. This is the foundation — nothing works without reliable event flow. Phase 2: DHL adapter (highest complexity due to bidirectional communication and SLA constraint) — start with the webhook fast-ack receiver, then the outbound client. Phase 3: SAP adapter (batch complexity but no real-time pressure) and Analytics adapter (simplest — stateless event transformation) in parallel. Validate each phase with integration tests that simulate external system behavior using WireMock (DHL), local SFTP (SAP), and embedded Kafka (Analytics).","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/domain/events/ — canonical event definitions (OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, ShipmentCreated, DeliveryAttempted, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed)","src/domain/order/ — Order aggregate, OrderRepository interface","src/domain/shipping/ — Shipment aggregate, ShipmentRepository interface","src/infrastructure/outbox/ — OutboxEntry entity, OutboxRelay (polling or CDC-based publisher)","src/infrastructure/eventbus/ — Internal event bus abstraction + implementation","src/adapters/sap/ — SAPOrderConfirmedHandler, XMLTransformer, SFTPClient, BatchScheduler","src/adapters/dhl/outbound/ — DHLShipmentClient, CircuitBreakerConfig, DHLRequestMapper","src/adapters/dhl/inbound/ — WebhookController, RawPayloadRepository, WebhookAsyncProcessor, DHLStatusTranslator","src/adapters/analytics/ — AnalyticsEventPublisher, KafkaProducerConfig, AnalyticsEventMapper","src/infrastructure/dlq/ — DeadLetterQueue per adapter, DLQMonitor","tests/integration/adapters/ — per-adapter integration tests with external system simulators"],"patterns_to_follow":"Hexagonal Architecture (Ports & Adapters) — domain at the center, adapters at the edges. Each adapter implements a port interface defined by the domain. Strict dependency rule: adapters depend on domain, never the reverse. Use dependency injection to wire adapters. All external communication happens through adapter implementations, never directly from domain code.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["DO NOT let DHL-specific data structures leak into the core domain — always translate through the ACL","DO NOT process DHL webhooks synchronously end-to-end — the 500ms SLA will be violated under load","DO NOT share a single consumer group across adapters — one slow adapter will block all others","DO NOT skip the transactional outbox — dual-writing to DB and event bus will eventually lose events","DO NOT couple the analytics Kafka producer to the internal event bus implementation — they are separate concerns even if both use Kafka","DO NOT batch DHL API calls to match SAP's 15-minute rhythm — DHL integration should be near-real-time per-event","DO NOT ignore would need API documentation to finalize the ACL translation layer","Whether the analytics platform requires exactly-once semantics or can tolerate at-least-once with
